the same as this untroubled uh this uncultivated mind is the trouble the mind now in some other suta the buddha gave a very clear simile for the five hindrances you know the buddha said suppose a man were to try to look for his reflection in water and he tries to see his reflection in a bowl of water and then the buddha says a person with strong this sensual desire is like a person trying to look for his reflection in a bowl of water which has in into which a lot of dye has been put d y e t y e the dyes different colors dark color of dye and then because of the different dyes that that water is no more clear so that when he tries to look for his reflection to see his own face he cannot see [Music] and then similarly for the second hindrance which is ill will the buddha says a person trying to look for his reflection in a bowl of water which is boiling and the water is boiling it is so trouble you look you're going to see your own reflection the third one is sloth and topper and then the sloth and topper the simile for it is like a bowl of water which has a lot of weeds a lot of water plants floating on the surface etc and because it is covered by these plants when you try to look for your reflection you can't see it that is a sloth and topper the fourth one is restlessness and worry buddha said a person trying to look for his reflection and the pool of water is stirred by the winds the winds are causing ripples waves on the surface so when you try to look for your reflection you cannot see that is restlessness and worry and then doubt the simile for doubt now is like uh this water is uh madhya and then you go and stir the mud and then turn up the mud and then this bowl of water you go and put in a dark place in a dark place you're trying to look for your reflection definitely you can't see so that is the simile for the doubt the the last hindu so here the buddha is talking about the the turbid state of water is the trouble the mind troubled mind is that minor which is full of the hindrances that's why you cannot see into water clearly that's why the the buddha said the water that is clear and still that is the similar for a cultivated mind very tranquil my mind is strong samadhi clear and bright and still then 1.5.8 [Music] monks i know not of any other single thing so quick to change as the mind in so much as in so much that it is no easy thing to illustrate how quick to change it is that's all this suta sutra and this is telling us that the mind changes extremely fast and because it changes extremely fast if we know we should use use it to good advantage for example if we have a good result a good intention that comes up for example some people think oh maybe i should become a monk so because there's a good intention you should quickly act if you don't act slowly yeah your mind will change the mind is fickle so when there's a good intention there's a good result we are afraid that it might change we quickly act on the other hand if you have a bad intention or bad [Music] resolve then you should not act on it for example anger rises and then you think you want to you if you react with a lot of anger and definitely you're going to do something which is unwholesome which is not wise because your state of mind is troubled and once you know that now then instead of acting you don't do anything just wait wait for the anger to subside and because the mind is quick to change it will subside if you just keep on waiting just do nothing just resolve not to act on it so that is using this with wisdom the fact that the mind can change very fast so today we are at suta number 1.5.9 the buddha said this mind monks is luminous but it is defiled by things that come from without and then 1.5.10 that mind monks is luminous but it is cleansed of genes that come from without these are the two sutas that i'm going to explain first these sutures are very interesting it is one of those who does that when somebody is new in the dhamma and he studies the sutas for the first time he does not notice the importance of a suta like this the buddha said this mine is luminous this mine here is a translation for chitta there are three [Music] translations for mine chitta is one vinyana consciousness which is normally translated as consciousness is another and mano or mana is the third line [Music] pinyana is normally used for consciousness that means being conscious of objects of the sixth sense laws and uh that is and uh mano is one of the sixth sense bases the thinking faculty mano is the thinking faculty and chitta here appears to be [Music] the translation is mine it is uh like more encompassing uh aspect of the mind there are various opinions about these three uh words some monks say that they all mean the same thing and some monks say that yeah different aspects of the mind anyway here it says that the cheetah is luminescent but it is defiled by things that come from outside from without you can also see that it is defiled by super imposed defilements now why this interest this suta is very interesting because there are two views on the final state of nirvana and those monks who uh study and follow the abhidharma and the commentaries they are of the opinion that in the state of nibana all the consciousness and the mind has stopped completely and there is nothing left but for the other view is the view of those monks who rely only on the su tesla and their view is a little similar to the hindu view of the ultimate state and uh the second viewer is that it is not that everything has seized entirely there seems uh to be something left according to the sutas because there is uh suta in the nikaya number 11 where the buddha said that the ultimate in the ultimate state uh there is still a light the mine is luminous uh so uh mentioned just now the kivada suta di nikaya number 11 the buddha said but you should not ask your question in this way where do the four great elements earth water fire wind seas without remainder instead this is how the question should have been put where do earth water fire and win no footing fine where are long and short small and great fair and foul where are nama rupa wholly destroyed and the answer is where consciousness is non-manifestative boundless or luminous that's where earth water fire and wind no footing fine they're both long and short small and great fair and foul their nama rupa are wholly destroyed with the cessation of consciousness this is all destroyed now so this is uh one suta where the buddha seems to talk of an ultimate state now where the normal consciousness the normal sex consciousness has ceased all together and yet there is still a statement which is boundless luminous uh non-manifestative consciousness and this non-manifestative consciousness is is believed to be similar to the kind of consciousness which the buddha calls unsupported consciousness unsupported consciousness which is completely different from the sixth normal consciousness the sixth normal consciousness they have to have an object as a condition for it to arise but not with the uh unsupported consciousness apathy tang vinayana so that is why this [Music] uh a viewer of the ultimate state is a bit controversial now the hindu concept is that they talk about an art man but then uh some monks and some buddhist monks say because they talk about admin that there is still a soul there is still an atman that is still what they call brahma in the ultimate state so they cling to the belief in the self but if you study the hindu teachings more deeply you find that their concept of atman is not the same as our buddhist concept of atta they also believe that there is no such thing as an ego that we take rebirth because of the clinging to an ego and they also say that you have to extinguish that ego to attain the ultimate state so their concept of atman if you study carefully they also call it brahma is quite different from the buddhist concept of atta it is something that is out of this world out of the whole of existence and some people uh who are not too familiar with the sutasa would be surprised to know that the buddha also calls the ultimate state of the buddha as the brahma body in the argania suta which is the diga nikaya number 27 the buddha said this designates the tata gata dhammakaya brahma so here in the nikaya number 27 the buddha says the tata gata which is the buddha is ultimately the dharmakaya or dharma body brahma kaya or brahma body and dharma buddha and dhamma become a brahma buddha brahma become or become brahma so this word that the buddha uses for the ultimate state which is brahma body and become brahma is not too different from the hindu concept of the ultimate reality which they call brahma and [Music] the the meaning of this suta appears to be and that this mind is basically bright and luminous but because of super enforced developments developments or things and that come from outside the external the mine is no more brighter it's no more bright this is why like for ordinary people when we close our eyes we see darkness all around the mind is dark whereas for an arahant it appears like when they even when they close their eyes and the mind is bright so there are some people like achan man in the thai tradition and the thai a chance they say that the original mind they talk about the original mind they say that the original mind is bright and then they say that the defilements are the normal sex consciousness because when the mind has the the six consciousness arise it means that there is an external object which attracts the attention of the six uh consciousness the six consciousness uh or one of them arise and so our energy is scattered out through the sixth sense doors because the energy is scattered out through the sixth sense doors so the the mind becomes scattered and when the mind becomes scattered it becomes uh sort of darken that is why when we practice one pointedness of mind we practice the janas and the samadhi that is taught by the buddha we don't allow the energy to go out through the five sense doors and and we even the sixth sense door the miner is put on one object one only one object and because of that the mind becomes one pointed becomes concentrated and the mind attains back that luminosity the brightness that is why jhana can be translated as a state of mental incandescence and so that is uh that is what i like to say about this suta that it is one of the important sutas which is used by those monks who follow the second opinion namely that the mind is originally bright and it can be turned to that original bright state of a different kind of consciousness and [Music] not the normal sex consciousness but we have to be very careful that when if we say that whether something exists in the ultimate state or something does not exist in the ultimate state because in the angutra nikaya chapter of the force the buddha said that concerning nibana do not say or say not that something exists and also say not that nothing exists it is it is something beyond the understanding of the mind because in the ultimate state and the mind has stopped working so the normal mind so you cannot perceive with the normal mind it appears that it can only be experienced but it cannot be known with the ordinary mind so now we come to the next suta which is 1.6.3 the buddha said monks if for just the lasting of a finger snap the monk indulges the thought of meta here translated as good will such a one is to be called among not empty of result is his jana he abides doing the master's bidding he is one who takes good advice and he eats the country's arms food to some purpose what then should i say of those who make much of such a thought that's the end of the suta here the buddha is trying to say that even good will or loving-kindness or impersonal love universal love if you even have uh a partner of meta then that manga is really is called a real monk and here the buddha also say it's not empty of result it is jana so from here it would appear that to practice meta you have to attain janafus to attain jhana then only you can radiate this metal now 1.6.8 the buddha said monks i know not of any other single thing of such power to cause the arising of evil states if not yet arisen or to cause the waning of good states if already arisen as negligence in him who is negligent evil states if not already arisen do arise and good states if a reason do win then the nexus says monks are 1.6.9 monks i know not of any other single thing of such power to cause the arising of good states if not yet arisen or to cause the waning of evil states if already arisen as earnestness in him who is earnest good states if not yet arisen do arise and evil states if arisen do win that earnestness is very important and negligence is uh something that we have to be very careful about negligence brings about evil states that means states of pain states of uh suffering and good states are states that you you you do not suffer you do not have pain and negligence you see leads to in careless negligence means carelessness basically not being serious and that leads to indolence being lazy and so it brings about all the bad states whereas if a person is sincere uh is earnest that means he's he has a lot of sincerity he's serious and that leads to a lot of effort and effort gives us brings us progress we get a lot of good states arising from it now the nexuta is 1.8 0.6 of slight account monks is the loss of such things as relatives miserable indeed among losses is the loss of wisdom and then 1.8 1.8.7 of slight account monks is the increase of such things as relatives chief of all the increases instead of wisdom therefore i say monks you should train yourselves thus we will increase in wisdom you must train yourselves to win that 10 1.8.8 of slight account monks is the loss of such things as wealth miserable indeed among losses is the loss of wisdom and 1.8.9 of slight account monks is the increase of such things as wealth chief of all the increases is that of wisdom therefore i say monks thus must you train yourselves we will increase in wisdom you must train yourselves to win that so these four sutas the buddha is trying to tell us that wisdom is more important than all the worldly things because among the worldly things for most people relatives are extremely important relatives and wealth these are the two most important things in the world for ordinary people but the buddha says all that is not important at all compared to wisdom wisdom is is the something that is much much more important than the worldly things because if we have wisdom then we would not uh continue on in this round of suffering and the round of ebirds and how do you get wisdom first we have to listen to the buddha's teachings like here the sutas because by listening to the buddha's teachings and then we understand what is important what is not important like here in the sutta and then also we know what to practice like keeping the precepts doing charity listening to the buddha's teachings the dhamma and then practice meditation etc so that is how we increase in our wisdom by understanding the buddha's teachings and the dhamma and then putting in it into practice especially meditation but i always like to say that meditation is like a university uh compared to the other aspects of the buddha's teachings like keeping the precepts uh making offerings and so it is um not something that we plunge into for a beginner it is uh best that we study the buddha's teachings first so that we have uh the theory before we go into practice just like we want to drive a car you have to learn the highway code first and learn how to steer etc before you go on the road otherwise a lot of us we get into wrong teachings and we can do it for many years before we realize that we have been wasting our time and then we stumble back into the correct teachings now one point 10 point 13. monks as a matter concerning the external i know not of any other single factor so conducive to great loss as friendship with the evil friendship with the evil indeed conduces to great loss 1.10.14 monks as a matter concerning the external i know not of any other single factor so conducive to great profit as friendship with the lovely indeed friendship with the lovely conduces to great profit now these two sutas are trying to tell us that uh especially in the spiritual part having a good friend kalyana mitta sometimes they translate in the mahayana as a good knowing advisor that means somebody on the spiritual path with you is very important this is especially so for someone who is not established on the path someone who has not who does not yet have unshakable faith in the buddha dharma and sangha if you and if somebody who is interested in the dharma but in the beginning in the initial [Music] steps of the path if he associate much with evil friends friends who are not interested in the dhamma friends who do not keep the sila the precepts who are not interested in to practice the spiritual path at all then it is for that person who is new on the path he is very easily influenced by his friends and he can uh lose his way along the path and go on the wrong track that's why the buddha says that friendship with somebody who's lovely and that means a spiritual friend who is well established on the spiritual path is very important for somebody new on the spiritual path in fact there's one suta where ananda said friendship with the lovely is half of the spiritual life and the buddha said something likes no and then i know friendship with the lovely is all to do with the spiritual path so the buddha is always saying that having a good friend on the spiritual path one who is established in the way is very important

items: the turbid nature of his mind
the untroubled nature of his mind

quiz.question: According to the text, what is the cause of a monk's inability to understand his own or others' teachings?
choices: The turbid nature of his mind
Inability to see fish in water

technique.title: Mind Cultivation Technique
steps: Identify the hindrances (sensual desire, ill will, sloth and torpor, restlessness and worry, doubt) in your mind.
Visualize water representing your mind. For each hindrance identified, imagine a specific disturbance to the water (e.g., dye for sensual desire, boiling for ill will).
Practice meditation focusing on calming these disturbances until the water becomes clear and still.

vow.text: Today I will cultivate my mind to be clear and still.
